figures.caption: Figure 1: SmoothECE for All Combined task, aggregated over all models.
Figure 8: Combination of tasks: Risk-coverage curves for the combinations of tasks described in Sec. 3.2, averaged across models. Higher is better.
Figure 9: Unanswerable requests: Risk-coverage curves for the combinations of answerable and unanswerable tasks described in Sec. 3.3, averaged across models. Higher is better.
Figure 10: SmoothECE for all tasks aggregated over models.
Table 7: Differences of mean AUROC of SEAST w.r.t. its values for J =10. T =1.0 fixed for all.
Table 6: Differences of mean AUROC of SEAST w.r.t. its values for T =1.0. J =10 fixed for all.
Table 2: Mean AUROC, ± mean standard error (mean taken over the eight LLMs) for all tasks from Sec. 3.

Figure 12: The algorithmic implementation of the classification of semantically meaningful tokens as defined in Sec. 4.3 for the JSON-style function call outputs by mistralai/ Ministral-8B-Instruct-2410.

Figure 3: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct response top token probabilities (lowest 5 bolded) for a question wrongly answered in the Natural Questions dataset. Question: When was the Wolf of Wall Street filmed? Ground truth answer: The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film) Filming began on August 8, 2012, in New York. Jonah Hill announced that his first day of shooting was September 4, 2012. Filming also took place in Closter, New Jersey and Harrison, New York. In January 2013, additional scenes were shot at a set built in an abandoned office building in Ardsley, New York. Scenes at the beach house were filmed in Sands Point, New York.
Figure 4: Individual tasks: AUROC ± standard error (bootstrap estimate with n = 1000) for the individual tasks described in Sec. 3.1. Boxplots summarize the variation across LLMs.
Figure 5: Combinations of tasks: AUROC ± std. error for the combinations of tasks described in Sec. 3.2. Boxplots summarize the variation across LLMs for a given UQ method.

Figure 11: The algorithmic implementation of the classification of semantically meaningful tokens as defined in Sec. 4.3 for the python list style function call outputs by the models qwen/qwen2.5-{0.5,3,7}B-instruct, qwen/qwen3-4B-instruct-2507, and google/gemma-2-9b-it as well as google/gemma-3-{4,12}b-it.

Figure 2: Top token probabilities for an incorrect Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct response (lowest 5 in bold) in the Multiple-Parallel task (details in Sec. A.4). Second-highest tokens shown in grey. Color coding explained in Sec. 4.3. The Ground truth answer does not contain the argument year=1882.

Table 1: Results: Mean AUROC ± mean standard error (mean over eight LLMs) for individual tasks, task combinations, and unanswerable requests described in Sec. 3.1, Sec. 3.2, and Sec. 3.3 respectively. The best result in green, underlined second best. For per-LLM results, see Figs. 4 to 6 (Sec. D.2).
Table 5: Effective sample sizes (number of successfully AST-parsed outputs) per model on the All Combined split (1000 datapoints total).

Figure 6: Unanswerable requests: AUROC ± standard error for the combinations of answerable and unanswerable tasks described in Sec. 3.3. Boxplots summarize the variation across LLMs for a given UQ method.
Figure 7: Individual tasks: Risk-coverage curves for the individual tasks described in Sec. 3.1, averaged across models. Higher is better.
